reasoning: The baseline probability of a US ally (NATO member or Major Non-NATO Ally) independently acquiring and finalizing full control over a nuclear weapon within the single year of 2025 is effectively zero. Historically, no US ally has established a new, independent nuclear arsenal since the UK and France did so decades ago, mainly because allies rely on the US nuclear umbrella (extended deterrence) and are signatories to the Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT). Developing an operational nuclear weapon requires significant, observable infrastructure (uranium enrichment or plutonium reprocessing) and testing, taking years to complete. None of the likely candidates (e.g., South Korea or Japan) have active weapons programs, and any decision to start one would trigger massive international fallout and take far past 2025 to yield a functional weapon. Shared weapons (like those hosted in Germany or Turkey) explicitly do not count under the resolution criteria. Consequently, I assign the minimum allowed probability (2%).
